source_text: “Drop it then,” said Mr. Cruncher; “I won’t have none of _your_ no harms. Get a top of that there seat, and look at the crowd.”
raw_output: “Then drop it,” said Mr. Cruncher; “I won’t have none of _your_ no harms. Get a top of that there seat, and look at the crowd.”
rewrite_text: “Then drop it,” said Mr. Cruncher; “I won’t have none of _your_ no harms. Get a top of that there seat, and look at the crowd.”

source_text: “So she will, upon my life; I never thought of that,” said Oak, pausing. “Yet I must tell him to-night, I suppose, for he’s working so far off, and leaves early.”
raw_output: “So she will, upon my life; I never thought of that,” said Oak, pausing. “Yet I must tell him tonight, I suppose, for he’s working so far off, and leaves early.”
rewrite_text: “So she will, upon my life; I never thought of that,” said Oak, pausing. “Yet I must tell him tonight, I suppose, for he’s working so far off, and leaves early.”

source_text: “His eyes was pretty shiny,” she confessed; “and he didn’t have no collar, though he went away with one. But mebbe he didn’t have more’n a couple of glasses.”
raw_output: “His eyes was pretty shiny,” she confessed; “and he didn’t have no collar, though he went away with one. But mebbe he didn’t have more’n a couple of glasses.”
rewrite_text: “His eyes was pretty shiny,” she confessed; “and he didn’t have no collar, though he went away with one. But mebbe he didn’t have more’n a couple of glasses.”

source_text: “’Tis right ye are, ma’am; ’tis by renting rooms we kape alive. Ye have the rale sense for business, ma’am. There be many people will rayjict the rentin’ of a room if they be tould a suicide has been after dyin’ in the bed of it.”
raw_output:
“’Tis right ye are, ma’am; ’tis by renting rooms we kape alive.  
Ye have the rale sense for business, ma’am.  
There be many people will rayjict the renting of a room  
if they be tould a suicide has been after dyin’  
in the bed of it.”
rewrite_text:
“’Tis right ye are, ma’am; ’tis by renting rooms we kape alive.  
Ye have the rale sense for business, ma’am.  
There be many people will rayjict the renting of a room  
if they be tould a suicide has been after dyin’  
in the bed of it.”

source_text: Inspired by recent work on discourse modeling (Kang et al., 2019; Nadeem et al., 2019) , we use language models to assess the narrative linearity of a story by measuring how sentences relate to their context in the story. We compare the likelihoods of sentences under two generative models ( Figure 2 ). The bag model makes the assumption that every sentence is drawn independently from the main theme of the story (represented by E). On the other hand, the chain model assumes that a story begins with a theme, and sentences linearly follow each other. 3 . ∆ l is computed as the difference in negative loglikelihoods between the bag and chain models:
raw_output: Motivated by recent studies on discourse modeling (Kang et al., 2019; Nadeem et al., 2019), we employ language models to evaluate the narrative linearity of a story by examining how sentences are connected to their surrounding context within the story. Specifically, we compare sentence likelihoods under two generative models (Figure 2). The bag model assumes that each sentence is independently generated from the story’s central theme, represented by E. In contrast, the chain model posits that a story starts from a theme and that sentences proceed in a linear sequence. 3. ∆l is calculated as the difference between the negative log-likelihoods of the bag and chain models:
rewrite_text: Motivated by recent studies on discourse modeling (Kang et al., 2019; Nadeem et al., 2019), we employ language models to evaluate the narrative linearity of a story by examining how sentences are connected to their surrounding context within the story. Specifically, we compare sentence likelihoods under two generative models (Figure 2). The bag model assumes that each sentence is independently generated from the story’s central theme, represented by E. In contrast, the chain model posits that a story starts from a theme and that sentences proceed in a linear sequence. 3. ∆l is calculated as the difference between the negative log-likelihoods of the bag and chain models:

source_text: To understand why SMART improves the performance, we analyze it on the ambiguous samples of MNLI dev set containing 3 classes, where each sample has 5 annotations. Based on the degree of agreement between these annotations, we divide the samples into 4 categories: 1) 5/0/0 all five annotations are the same; 2) 4/1/0 four annotations are the same; 3) 3/2/0 three annotations are the same and the other two annotations are the same; 4) 3/1/1 three annotations are the same and the other two annotations are different. Figure 2 summarizes the results in terms of both accuracy and KL-divergence:
raw_output: To investigate why SMART enhances performance, we examine the ambiguous examples in the MNLI development set with three classes, where each instance is associated with five annotations. According to the extent of agreement among these annotations, we partition the examples into four categories: 1) 5/0/0, all five annotations are identical; 2) 4/1/0, four annotations are identical; 3) 3/2/0, three annotations are identical and the remaining two annotations are identical; and 4) 3/1/1, three annotations are identical and the other two annotations differ. Figure 2 reports the results in terms of both accuracy and KL divergence:
rewrite_text: To investigate why SMART enhances performance, we examine the ambiguous examples in the MNLI development set with three classes, where each instance is associated with five annotations. According to the extent of agreement among these annotations, we partition the examples into four categories: 1) 5/0/0, all five annotations are identical; 2) 4/1/0, four annotations are identical; 3) 3/2/0, three annotations are identical and the remaining two annotations are identical; and 4) 3/1/1, three annotations are identical and the other two annotations differ. Figure 2 reports the results in terms of both accuracy and KL divergence:

source_text: e −y ij * F (q i ,a i ,r ij ) ), where y ij is the label for r ij . Softmax: We will discuss in §2.3 that annotators are expected to miss a few good responses since good and bad answers are often very similar (may only differ by a single preposition or pronoun). Therefore, we explore a ranking objective that calculates errors based on the margin with which incorrect responses are ranked above correct ones (Collins and Koo, 2005) . Without loss of generality, we assume r i1 to be better than all other responses for (q i , a i ). Since the model F () should rank r i1 higher than all other responses, we use the margin error (F ) . We experiment with the following feature based and neural models with the two loss functions: Language Model Baseline: The responses are ranked using the normalized probabilities from a 3-gram LM trained on the Gigaword corpus with modified Kneser-Ney smoothing. 3 The response with the highest score is classified as 1 and others as 0. Linear Model: A linear classifier using features inspired by Heilman and Smith (2010) and Wan et al. (2006) , who have implemented similar linear models for other sentence pair classification tasks. Specifically, we use the following features:

We therefore consider a ranking objective that measures mistakes according to the margin by which incorrect responses are placed above correct ones (Collins and Koo, 2005). Without loss of generality, we assume that ri1 is superior to all other responses for (qi, ai). Because the model F() should assign ri1 a higher rank than all remaining responses, we adopt the margin error (F). Linear Model: A linear classifier with features motivated by Heilman and Smith (2010) and Wan et al. (2006), who developed comparable linear models for other sentence-pair classification tasks. In particular, we employ the following features:

source_text: GAN-BERT: Semi-supervised BERT with Adversarial LearningBidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT)(Devlin et al., 2019) belongs to the family of the so-called transfer learning methods, where a model is first pre-trained on general tasks and then fine-tuned on the final target tasks. In Computer Vision, transfer learning has been shown beneficial in many different tasks, i.e., pre-training a neural network model on a known task, followed by a fine-tuning stage on a (different) target task (see, for example,(Girshick et al., 2013)). BERT
raw_output: GAN-BERT: Semi-supervised BERT with Adversarial Learning Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) (Devlin et al., 2019) is part of the class of so-called transfer learning approaches, in which a model is first pre-trained on general-purpose tasks and then fine-tuned on the final target tasks. In computer vision, transfer learning has been demonstrated to be advantageous across many different tasks, namely by pre-training a neural network on a known task and then applying a fine-tuning phase on a (different) target task (see, for instance, (Girshick et al., 2013)). BERT
rewrite_text: GAN-BERT: Semi-supervised BERT with Adversarial Learning Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) (Devlin et al., 2019) is part of the class of so-called transfer learning approaches, in which a model is first pre-trained on general-purpose tasks and then fine-tuned on the final target tasks. In computer vision, transfer learning has been demonstrated to be advantageous across many different tasks, namely by pre-training a neural network on a known task and then applying a fine-tuning phase on a (different) target task (see, for instance, (Girshick et al., 2013)). BERT

source_text: We utilize the, now common, evaluation technique of checking whether a model assigns higher probability to grammatical sentences compared to ungrammatical sentences (Linzen et al., 2016) . However, we extend beyond binary grammaticality. Real world applications demand that our models not only know the difference between valid and invalid sentences; they must also be able to correctly prioritize simultaneous valid interpretations (Lau et al., 2017) . In this paper, we investigate whether neural networks can in fact prioritize simultaneous interpretations in a human-like way. In particular, we probe the biases of neural networks for ambiguous relative clause (RC) attachments, such as the following:
raw_output: We employ the now-standard evaluation method of testing whether a model assigns higher probability to grammatical sentences than to ungrammatical ones (Linzen et al., 2016). However, our analysis goes beyond binary grammaticality. In real-world applications, models must not only distinguish well-formed from ill-formed sentences; they must also be able to appropriately rank competing valid interpretations (Lau et al., 2017). In this paper, we examine whether neural networks can in fact prioritize competing interpretations in a human-like manner. Specifically, we investigate the biases of neural networks toward ambiguous relative clause (RC) attachments, such as the following:
